title: Economic integration and the WTO
summary: When countries deepen their trade ties they move along a spectrum of economic integration, from a loose preferential deal all the way to a full monetary union. This lesson builds that spectrum step by step, identifies the single feature that changes at each stage, distinguishes trade creation from trade diversion, weighs the benefits and costs of sharing a currency, and sets out how the World Trade Organization (WTO) tries to keep global trade open and rules-based.
learningObjectives: Place the forms of economic integration on a spectrum and state the single feature that changes at each step from a preferential trade agreement to a monetary/economic union.
Explain, at the concept level, the difference between trade creation and trade diversion and why it matters for welfare.
Evaluate the main economic benefits and costs of joining a monetary union.
Describe the role of the WTO in trade liberalisation, dispute settlement, and multilateral trade rounds.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Countries teaming up, step by step
simpleExplanation.summary: Economic integration is countries agreeing to trade more freely with each other. There is a ladder of deals: each rung removes one more barrier between members. The WTO is the global referee that tries to keep trade open and settle disputes between all countries.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a group of neighbours who cook together. First they just swap a few ingredients cheaply (a preferential deal). Then they share everything in their kitchens for free (a free trade area). Next they agree to charge outsiders the same price for anything they sell (a customs union). Then they let each other walk in and use each other's kitchens and hire each other's help freely (a common market). Finally they pool their money into one shared wallet (a monetary union). Each step gives up a bit more independence in return for closer cooperation.
steps: Name the form of integration and state the one feature that defines it — especially whether there is a common external tariff.
Trade creation is good: joining lets a country switch from expensive home production to a cheaper partner, raising efficiency.
Trade diversion is the risk: the common external tariff can push trade away from the cheapest world producer towards a costlier member, lowering efficiency.
A monetary union adds a shared currency: lower transaction costs and price transparency, but the loss of an independent monetary policy and exchange-rate adjustment.

flashcards.back: The process by which countries coordinate policy to reduce and ultimately remove barriers to the flow of goods, services and factors of production between them.
A group of countries that have formally agreed to reduce trade barriers between themselves to gain from closer economic cooperation.
The loosest form of integration: members lower — but do not eliminate — barriers on selected goods or services with each other. Barriers remain, just reduced.
Members remove tariffs and quotas on trade between themselves, BUT each member keeps its OWN separate external trade policy towards non-members. No common external tariff.
A free trade area PLUS a common external tariff (CET): every member charges the same tariff on imports from outside the union. The CET is the key feature that distinguishes it from an FTA.
A customs union PLUS free movement of factors of production — labour and capital can move freely between members.
A common market PLUS a shared currency and a single central bank running one monetary policy for all members. An economic union adds harmonised fiscal/economic policy.
A welfare GAIN: joining a bloc lets consumption shift from a higher-cost domestic producer to a lower-cost producer inside the bloc. Production moves to the more efficient source.

A welfare LOSS: because of the common external tariff, trade shifts AWAY from a lower-cost producer outside the bloc towards a higher-cost producer inside it. Production moves to a less efficient source.
A single tariff applied by all members of a customs union to imports from non-members. Its existence is exactly what separates a customs union from a free trade area.

flashcards.front: One benefit of a monetary union
flashcards.back: Lower transaction costs (no currency conversion) and price transparency across members, which encourages trade, investment and competition within the union.

flashcards.front: One cost of a monetary union
flashcards.back: Loss of an independent monetary policy and of exchange-rate adjustment: a member can no longer set its own interest rates or let its currency depreciate to respond to a shock affecting it alone.

The global body that sets and enforces the rules of international trade, hosts negotiations to liberalise trade, and settles disputes between members. Its core principle is non-discrimination (most-favoured-nation).
A multi-year set of multilateral negotiations under the WTO (or previously the GATT) aimed at lowering trade barriers across many countries and sectors at once (e.g. the Doha Round).

quickCheck.prompt: The single feature that distinguishes a customs union from a free trade area is that a customs union has:
options: Free movement of labour between members
A common external tariff on imports from non-members
quickCheck.answer: A common external tariff on imports from non-members

quickCheck.prompt: Trade shifts from a low-cost producer OUTSIDE the bloc to a higher-cost producer INSIDE it because of the common external tariff. This is:
By joining a monetary union, a member country MOST clearly gives up its ability to:
options: Set its own independent monetary policy and use exchange-rate adjustment
quickCheck.answer: Set its own independent monetary policy and use exchange-rate adjustment

quickCheck.prompt: Which sequence correctly orders the forms of integration from LEAST to MOST integrated?
options: Customs union → free trade area → common market → monetary union
Free trade area → customs union → common market → monetary union
Common market → customs union → free trade area → monetary union
Monetary union → common market → customs union → free trade area
quickCheck.answer: Free trade area → customs union → common market → monetary union

quickCheck.prompt: A country switches from producing a good expensively at home to importing it more cheaply from a new bloc partner. This is:

faq.q: What is the quickest way to tell a free trade area from a customs union?
faq.a: Look at what happens at the OUTSIDE border. In a free trade area, members trade freely with each other but each keeps its own separate tariff on non-members — so a good entering different members faces different external tariffs. In a customs union, every member applies the SAME common external tariff to non-members. That single common external tariff is the whole difference, and it is the point examiners test most often.

faq.q: Why is trade creation 'good' and trade diversion 'bad'?
faq.a: Both change where a good is produced. Trade creation moves production to a MORE efficient (lower-cost) source — the world uses fewer resources to make the same output, so welfare rises. Trade diversion moves production to a LESS efficient (higher-cost) source that only looks cheaper because the common external tariff penalises the true low-cost producer — so real resources are wasted and welfare falls. Whether a bloc is beneficial overall depends on whether trade creation outweighs trade diversion.

faq.q: If a monetary union has clear benefits, why doesn't every country join one?
faq.a: Because the central cost is steep: a member surrenders its own interest-rate policy and can no longer let its exchange rate move to cushion a shock that hits it alone. If one member falls into recession while others boom, the single central bank cannot set an interest rate that suits everyone, and the struggling member cannot devalue to regain competitiveness. Whether membership is worth it depends on how similar members' economies are and how easily labour and fiscal support can move to absorb shocks instead.

sections.content: You have almost certainly used products that reached you through a trading bloc — the European Union, or the agreement linking the United States, Mexico and Canada. These are examples of economic integration: countries deliberately lowering the barriers between them to trade and cooperate more closely. In this lesson we build the spectrum of integration one step at a time, ask whether closer ties actually make countries better off (trade creation versus trade diversion), weigh the special case of sharing a currency, and meet the global referee that sits above all of it — the World Trade Organization.
What economic integration means

Economic integration is an agreement among countries — usually in the same region — to reduce and eventually remove the barriers to trade and to the movement of factors of production between them. The aim is greater efficiency through specialisation and competition, larger markets that allow economies of scale, and often closer political ties. The deeper the integration, the more each member gains from cooperation but the more national economic sovereignty it gives up. That trade-off — interdependence in exchange for autonomy — is the thread running through the whole topic.
The spectrum of integration: one new feature at each step

It is tempting to memorise five definitions in isolation, but the smarter approach is to see them as a spectrum. Start with the loosest arrangement and add exactly ONE new feature at each step. If you can name the feature that gets added, you can never confuse two neighbouring forms — and that is precisely where marks are won and lost.

items: **Preferential trade agreement (PTA):** members lower barriers on SOME goods, but barriers still exist. *New feature: reduced (not removed) barriers on selected goods.*

**Free trade area (FTA):** members remove tariffs and quotas on trade between themselves, but each keeps its OWN external policy towards non-members. *New feature: zero internal barriers — but no common external tariff.*
**Customs union:** an FTA plus a common external tariff (CET) — every member charges the same tariff on non-members. *New feature: the common external tariff.*
**Common market:** a customs union plus free movement of factors of production — labour and capital can move freely between members. *New feature: free movement of labour and capital.*

**Monetary/economic union:** a common market plus a shared currency and a single central bank running one monetary policy. *New feature: a common currency and shared monetary policy* (an economic union goes further and harmonises fiscal and economic policy too).

sections.content: Read down that list and only one thing changes each time. The most heavily tested step is the jump from free trade area to customs union: the ONLY difference is the common external tariff. In a free trade area, members trade freely with one another but each sets its own tariff on the rest of the world, so the same imported good can face different tariffs depending on which member it enters. In a customs union, all members present a single common external tariff to non-members.

sections.question:
Country A and Country B remove all tariffs on trade between themselves. Country A charges a 10% tariff on cars from the rest of the world, while Country B charges 25% on cars from the rest of the world.

(a) What form of integration is this — a free trade area or a customs union? Justify your answer.
(b) A car importer wants to sell into both A and B. Explain one practical problem this arrangement creates that a customs union would remove.
sections.solution:
**(a)** This is a **free trade area**, not a customs union. The defining test is the external tariff: A and B apply DIFFERENT tariffs to non-members (10% versus 25%). A customs union would require a single COMMON external tariff applied by both. Because there is free internal trade but no common external tariff, the arrangement sits at the free-trade-area rung of the spectrum.

**(b)** Because A's external tariff (10%) is lower than B's (25%), an importer could bring cars from the rest of the world into A cheaply, then move them tariff-free into B — sidestepping B's higher tariff. This is the classic problem of trade deflection, and it forces free trade areas to police 'rules of origin' to check where goods really came from. A customs union removes the problem entirely: with one common external tariff, there is no cheaper door to enter through, so goods can move between members without origin checks. This is a concrete reason customs unions reduce administrative friction relative to free trade areas.

sections.content: Do trading blocs help? Trade creation versus trade diversion

Joining a bloc changes WHERE goods are produced, and that can either raise or lower efficiency. Economists separate the two effects. Trade creation is the beneficial effect: membership lets a country stop producing a good expensively at home and instead import it from a lower-cost partner inside the bloc. Production shifts to a more efficient source, resources are freed for better uses, and welfare rises. Trade diversion is the harmful effect: because the common external tariff penalises producers outside the bloc, a country may switch from the world's genuinely lowest-cost producer to a higher-cost partner inside the bloc. Production shifts to a LESS efficient source — it only looks cheaper because of the tariff — so real resources are wasted and welfare falls.

At the concept level, the key insight is comparative: it is not whether a country imports more, but whether imports come from a MORE efficient source (creation) or a LESS efficient one (diversion). A bloc is beneficial overall only if the gains from trade creation outweigh the losses from trade diversion — which is why the answer to 'are trading blocs good?' is always 'it depends'.

sections.question:
Before joining any bloc, Country X buys all its coffee from Vietnam, the world's lowest-cost producer, because Vietnamese coffee is cheapest even after X's tariff. X then joins a customs union whose common external tariff applies to Vietnam but not to a fellow member, Colombia. Colombia's coffee is more expensive to produce than Vietnam's, but once the common external tariff is added to Vietnamese coffee, Colombian coffee becomes the cheaper option for X's consumers, who switch to it.

Meanwhile, X used to grow some of its own coffee at high cost behind the tariff; after joining, it stops home production and imports that portion from cheaper Colombia instead.

Identify which effect is trade creation and which is trade diversion, and state the welfare consequence of each.
sections.solution:
There are TWO distinct shifts here — separate them.

1. **Switching from home-grown coffee to imported Colombian coffee = TRADE CREATION.** X was producing coffee itself at high cost; it now imports from Colombia, a lower-cost source than its own farmers. Production has moved to a MORE efficient source, so this part raises welfare — resources previously tied up in inefficient domestic coffee growing are freed for uses where X is more competitive.

2. **Switching from Vietnamese imports to Colombian imports = TRADE DIVERSION.** Vietnam is genuinely the lowest-cost world producer; Colombia only wins because the common external tariff is levied on Vietnam but not on a fellow member. Production has moved to a LESS efficient source, so this part LOWERS welfare — the world now uses more resources to grow the same coffee, and X's government also loses the tariff revenue it previously collected on Vietnamese coffee.

**Overall:** whether joining the union benefits X depends on the relative SIZE of the trade creation gain versus the trade diversion loss. This is exactly the comparison an evaluation question is testing: more trade is not automatically better — what matters is whether trade moves to a more or less efficient source.

sections.content: The single most common error on this topic is calling any increase in trade 'trade creation'. It is not. Always ask: did trade move to a MORE efficient producer (creation, welfare up) or a LESS efficient one that only looks cheap because of the tariff (diversion, welfare down)? Naming the direction of the efficiency shift is what earns the analysis marks.
The special case of monetary union: what you gain and what you give up

A monetary union goes beyond free trade in goods and factors: members abandon their national currencies for a single shared currency managed by one central bank. This brings real benefits, but it also forces members to surrender tools they may badly want during a downturn. A strong evaluation weighs both sides rather than listing one.

items: **Benefit — lower transaction costs:** with one currency, firms and travellers no longer pay to convert money or hedge against currency swings when trading between members, which lowers the cost of cross-border business.

**Benefit — price transparency:** prices across members are quoted in the same currency, so buyers can compare directly. This sharpens competition, discourages price discrimination and encourages trade and investment within the union.
**Benefit — exchange-rate stability within the union:** members can no longer see their exchange rates move against each other, removing a source of uncertainty for intra-union trade and investment.

**Cost — loss of independent monetary policy:** a single central bank sets ONE interest rate for all members. A member in recession cannot cut its own rate; a member overheating cannot raise its own — the 'one size fits all' policy may suit no one exactly.

**Cost — loss of exchange-rate adjustment:** a member hit by a shock affecting it alone can no longer let its currency depreciate to restore competitiveness. Adjustment must instead come through slower, more painful routes such as falling wages or higher unemployment.

**Cost — reduced sovereignty and asymmetric shocks:** because members give up these tools, a union works best when members' economies are similar and when labour and fiscal transfers can move to cushion shocks — conditions that do not always hold.

sections.question:
Country Y is in a monetary union. A collapse in demand for its main export — of a kind that does NOT affect the other members — pushes Y into recession while the rest of the union grows steadily.

(a) Explain why Y cannot use the two macro tools it would have had OUTSIDE the union.
(b) State one benefit Y still enjoys from membership despite the recession.
sections.solution:
**(a)** Outside the union, Y could respond to a recession affecting it alone in two ways it has now given up:

- *Independent monetary policy:* it could cut its own interest rate to stimulate borrowing and spending. Inside the union, the single central bank sets ONE rate for all members. Because the rest of the union is growing, that rate will be set for average conditions and will be too HIGH for a country in recession — Y cannot cut it alone.

- *Exchange-rate adjustment:* outside, Y's currency could depreciate, making its exports cheaper abroad and helping demand recover. Inside the union there is a single shared currency, so Y cannot devalue against fellow members. Adjustment must instead come through 'internal devaluation' — falling wages and prices — which is slow and typically raises unemployment first.

This is the core cost of monetary union: the loss of BOTH the monetary-policy lever and the exchange-rate lever precisely when an asymmetric shock makes a member most want them.

**(b)** Y still benefits from lower transaction costs and price transparency on its trade with the rest of the union: even in recession, its firms trade with fellow members without currency-conversion costs or exchange-rate uncertainty, which supports whatever export demand remains. (Accept also: continued access to the single market / no exchange-rate risk within the union.)

sections.content: The World Trade Organization: the global referee

Trading blocs are, by design, discriminatory: they give members better terms than non-members. Sitting above them is the WTO, the only global body dealing with the rules of trade between nations. Its purpose is to keep trade flowing as smoothly, predictably and freely as possible, and its foundation is non-discrimination — the 'most-favoured-nation' principle that a country should treat all its WTO trading partners equally, and 'national treatment', that imports should be treated no worse than domestic goods once inside a market.

items: **Trade liberalisation:** the WTO provides the forum where member governments negotiate agreements that lower tariffs and other barriers, spreading the gains from freer trade across many countries at once.

**Multilateral trade rounds:** major reductions are negotiated in multi-year 'rounds' (such as the Doha Round) covering many countries and sectors together — though reaching consensus among over 160 members has become very difficult.

**Dispute settlement:** the WTO runs a structured, rules-based system for resolving trade disagreements, so conflicts are settled through agreed procedures rather than through tit-for-tat retaliation and trade wars.
**Monitoring and capacity building:** it oversees how members implement the rules, requires them to notify their trade policies, and gives technical assistance to help developing countries trade.

sections.content: A classic evaluation link: trading blocs are inherently discriminatory (they favour members), which sits awkwardly with the WTO's most-favoured-nation principle of treating all partners equally. You can score well by discussing whether regional blocs are a 'building block' towards global free trade (they get countries used to open markets) or a 'stumbling block' (they divert trade and fragment the world into rival camps).
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Confusing a free trade area with a customs union** — the ONLY defining difference is the common external tariff. A free trade area has none; a customs union has one. Say this explicitly.

**Calling any rise in trade 'trade creation'** — trade creation means trade shifts to a MORE efficient producer. If it shifts to a less efficient member because of the common external tariff, that is trade DIVERSION and a welfare LOSS.
**Treating trade diversion as always outweighing creation (or vice versa)** — the net effect is ambiguous; a good answer says it DEPENDS on the relative size of the two.
**Listing only the benefits of a monetary union** — the loss of independent monetary policy and exchange-rate adjustment is the central cost. Omitting it caps evaluation marks.
**Saying the opportunity cost of a monetary union is 'the shared currency'** — the real cost is the surrender of policy TOOLS (own interest rate, own exchange rate), not the currency itself.

**Confusing the WTO with a trading bloc** — the WTO is a global, non-discriminatory rule-setter and dispute-settler; a trading bloc is a preferential, discriminatory regional deal. They pull in different directions.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB markband

sections.question: Paper 1, part (a): Explain the difference between a free trade area and a customs union, and one economic benefit and one economic cost of joining a monetary union. [10 marks]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**

A free trade area and a customs union are two rungs on the spectrum of economic integration, and the single feature that separates them is the treatment of non-members. In a free trade area, member countries remove all tariffs and quotas on trade BETWEEN themselves, but each member keeps its OWN independent external trade policy — so different members can charge different tariffs on the same good imported from the rest of the world. A customs union goes one step further: it is a free trade area PLUS a common external tariff (CET), meaning every member applies the SAME tariff to imports from non-members. That common external tariff is the whole difference — it removes the need for rules-of-origin checks between members and presents a single trade front to the outside world.

A monetary union is a much deeper form of integration in which members share a single currency managed by one central bank. One economic BENEFIT of joining is lower transaction costs together with price transparency: firms and consumers trading between members no longer pay to convert currencies or hedge against exchange-rate movements, and prices are quoted in a single currency so buyers can compare them directly. This encourages trade, investment and competition within the union, raising efficiency.

One economic COST of joining is the loss of an independent monetary policy and of exchange-rate adjustment. A single central bank sets ONE interest rate for the whole union, so a member in recession cannot cut its own rate, and no member can let its exchange rate depreciate to restore competitiveness after a shock that affects it alone. Adjustment must then come through slower, more painful routes such as falling wages and higher unemployment. This is why a monetary union suits members only when their economies are similar enough that a common policy fits them all.

*(A diagram is not essential for this question — the demands are conceptual — though a simple spectrum sketch or a trade-creation/diversion diagram could be used to support the explanation where appropriate.)*

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 1 part (a), out of 10):**

- **9–10:** the demands of the question are addressed, relevant economic theory is FULLY explained, relevant terms are used appropriately THROUGHOUT, and, where appropriate, a relevant diagram is included and FULLY explained.
- **7–8:** the demands are explained, and terms are mostly used appropriately.
- **5–6:** the demands are partially addressed and PARTLY explained, with some appropriate terms.
- **3–4:** the demands are DESCRIBED, with some appropriate terms.
- **1–2:** the response is not relevant to the question.

Why this answer reaches the top (9–10) band:
- ✅ **All demands addressed:** the free-trade-area/customs-union difference, one benefit AND one cost of monetary union — none skipped.
- ✅ **Theory FULLY explained:** the common external tariff is not just named but shown to be the defining difference; the benefit and the cost are each explained through their mechanism (transaction costs/transparency; loss of interest-rate and exchange-rate tools), not merely stated.
- ✅ **Terms used appropriately THROUGHOUT:** free trade area, customs union, common external tariff, monetary union, transaction costs, price transparency, monetary policy, exchange-rate adjustment.
- ✅ **Diagram:** a diagram is NOT essential here because the question is conceptual — and the top band only requires one 'where appropriate'. The answer notes where one could support the explanation, which is enough. Marks are not lost for omitting a diagram on a question that does not demand it.

An answer that merely DESCRIBED the two blocs ('an FTA has free trade, a customs union is a group of countries') and only NAMED a benefit and a cost without explaining the mechanism would sit in the 3–4 band. Explaining WHY — the common external tariff as the dividing line, and the lost policy tools as the real cost — is exactly what our marking engine rewards.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same official markband — telling you which band you reached and why. The habit that moves you up the bands here: EXPLAIN the mechanism (the common external tariff as the dividing line; the lost policy tools as the real cost of a monetary union), don't just describe the blocs.
Key concepts in this lesson

This lesson develops two of the course's key concepts. **Interdependence** runs through every rung of the integration spectrum: the deeper the bloc, the more each member's economy relies on — and is shaped by — the others, which is both the source of the gains and the reason a shared monetary policy can hurt. **Efficiency** is the yardstick for judging it all: trade creation raises efficiency by moving production to lower-cost sources, trade diversion lowers it, and the WTO's push for liberalisation aims to spread efficiency gains globally. Keep both concepts in view — evaluation questions reward candidates who tie the mechanics back to them.
Where this leads

Economic integration is the applied face of ideas you have already met: comparative advantage explains why removing barriers can raise output, and the analysis of tariffs underlies trade creation and diversion. It also sets up the debates ahead — exchange-rate policy, the causes of current-account imbalances, and how developing economies weigh openness against protection. The recurring question is the one you met here: does closer integration move the world's production to more efficient sources, and is the loss of policy independence worth the gains from cooperation?

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 (a) answer marked: FTA vs customs union, plus a benefit and a cost of monetary union

items.label: The Economist explains: What is a customs union?
